title: Interacting causes of mini-grid failure - Nature Energy
text:
Access through your institution

Buy or subscribe

Mini grids can enable rural electrification, but their long-term operation and maintenance are impacted by complex interacting factors. Problems associated with finance, governance, and trust can contribute to the failure of community-managed mini grids. Such difficulties have led to discussion over the long-term sustainability of these grids and the interaction between different aspects of infrastructure and external factors. Now, Irene F. Ngoti, Lars Holstenkamp and Tobias Klaus from institutions in Tanzania and Germany have examined four solar-based community-managed mini grids in Tanzania to understand what leads to mini grid failure.Data on different components of mini-grid infrastructure — resources, public infrastructure systems and providers, and resource users — and local conditions were collected through semi-structured interviews and focus group discussions. Resource users described problems with governance breakdown, trust erosion, financing performance, and political interference. Analysing this information with causal loop diagrams illustrated that these four recurring themes interact and self-reinforce to cause grid failure. For example, issues around legal ownership and local politics impact tariff payment and trust; financing issues in turn impact maintenance. The findings suggest that clearer mini-grid ownership, user education, and minimal involvement of local politics could support long-term sustainability of rural community-managed mini grids.
